articles.title: Punk-Rock Chicks and Hidden Stories: Inside the Smithsonian Photo Contest Winners
articles.summary: What do baby birds and punk rockers have in common? Spiky mohawks! Three baby egrets with wild, unruly feathers won the grand prize in the Smithsonian Magazine photo contest. Photographer Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old vet from Pennsylvania, snapped 'The Punk Rockers' at a rookery under a ramp in Ocean City, New Jersey. The tiny chicks, each no bigger than a golf ball, pecked at each other among 50 to 70 nests. Over 17,000 photos from 108 countries entered the contest. Other winners show a barefoot devotee at a Hindu festival, a Navajo teen during her coming-of-age ceremony, and a Mundari child cleaning a bull's horns. Burke hopes her photo inspires people to protect these amazing creatures.

articles.title: ‘Like a Tree Rooted in Soil’: London Exhibition Gives Voice to Southern Lebanon
articles.summary: What if a place you love kept getting hurt, over and over? That's the story an exhibition in London called 'Forget Me Not' tells about southern Lebanon. Curators Rasha Kotaiche and Ali Abou Khalil used photos, videos, and children's drawings to show the region's long history of conflict. One video, 'What Remains' by Nor Nsralla, shares memories from people who lived through Israel's 2024 invasion. A resident explains their deep bond: 'We are attached to this land just like a tree is rooted in its soil.' The show also features drawings by Lebanese kids and pictures of pomegranates from Bint Jbeil. The curators want to replace old stories with ones written by the people themselves. Even after a ceasefire in…

articles.title: Art World Olympics: Drama, Boycotts, and a Historic First at the 2026 Venice Biennale
articles.summary: The Venice Biennale, known as the 'Art World Olympics,' is back with drama and historic firsts. This year's main show, 'In Minor Keys,' was curated by Koyo Kouoh, the first African woman ever chosen for the job. After she died suddenly, her team kept her vision alive with 110 artists like Nick Cave and Wangechi Mutu. Qatar will build a new permanent pavilion in the Giardini—the first in 30 years—as it moves away from oil. First-timers include Vietnam, Somalia, Guinea, and Sierra Leone. But controversy stole the spotlight: South Africa withdrew after canceling a video about Gaza victims, and judges won't consider pavilions from countries whose leaders face war crimes charges. The US pavilion got a new artist after Barbara…
